tags: Rank of a set of vectors

content.problemStatement:
An array of integers is called good if all its elements are perfect squares.

You are given an array AAA of NNN integers. In one move, you can do the following:


Pick a subset of indices of the array, say {i1,i2,…,ik}\{i_1, i_2, \ldots, i_k\}{i1​,i2​,…,ik​} where 1≤i1<i2<…<ik≤N1 \leq i_1 \lt i_2 \lt \ldots \lt i_k \leq N1≤i1​<i2​<…<ik​≤N
Next, pick an integer X>0X \gt 0X>0
Finally, multiply the value at each chosen index by XXX, i.e, set AijA_{i_j}Aij​​ to Aij⋅XA_{i_j} \cdot XAij​​⋅X for each 1≤j≤k1 \leq j \leq k1≤j≤k
Find the **minimum** number of moves required to make the array good.

**Note:** The value of XXX is fixed for a given move, but can vary between moves.


Input Format

The first line of input contains an integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
Each test case contains two lines of input.
The first line of each test case contains NNN, the size of the array.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.


Output Format
For each testcase, output in a single line the **minimum** number of moves required to make the array good.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 10^4
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains an integer N, the size of the array. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the minimum number of moves required to make all array elements perfect squares.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: One possible sequence of moves is:

Multiply the first element by 151515
Multiply the second element by 222

The array is now [225,4,4]=[152,22,22][225, 4, 4] = [15^2, 2^2, 2^2][225,4,4]=[152,22,22], which is good.
Test case 333: One possible sequence of moves is:

Multiply the first and third elements by 888
Multiply the second and third elements by 333
Multiply the first element by 202020

The array is now [1600,9,144]=[402,32,122][1600, 9, 144] = [40^2, 3^2, 12^2][1600,9,144]=[402,32,122], which is good.
Test case 444: The array is already good, since [4,9]=[22,32][4, 9] = [2^2, 3^2][4,9]=[22,32], so no moves need to be made.

solution.approach:
For each number Ai factorise it and keep only the primes whose exponent is odd – this gives a binary vector (over GF(2)) describing which prime‑exponents are currently odd. Multiplying a chosen subset of elements by a number X adds the parity vector of X to every selected element (XOR operation). Hence a move corresponds to choosing a vector v and XOR‑adding it to any subset of the current vectors. The whole process is equivalent to representing every initial vector as a XOR‑sum of a small set of move‑vectors. The smallest possible number of move‑vectors is exactly the rank of the set of initial vectors (the size of a basis). Therefore the answer for a test case is the rank of the collection of parity vectors (ignoring the zero vector, which already represents a perfect square).

Algorithm per test case:
1. Pre‑compute all primes up to √10^8 = 10^4 using a sieve.
2. For each Ai, factorise using the prime list. For every prime with an odd exponent toggle its presence in the vector. If after the loop a remainder > 1 remains, it is a prime with exponent 1 (odd) and is added as a new dimension.
3. Represent each vector as a Python integer (bit i corresponds to the i‑th distinct prime encountered). Store only non‑zero vectors.
4. Perform Gaussian elimination over GF(2) on these integers to obtain the rank:
   - Keep a list `basis`.
   - For each mask, repeatedly eliminate its highest set bit using existing basis elements; if it becomes zero it is dependent, otherwise insert it into the basis.
5. The answer is the size of `basis`.

Complexities:
- Factorisation of one number uses at most the 1229 primes ≤ 10^4 → O(√Ai / log) ≈ O(1229).
- Overall factorisation O(N * 1229) ≤ 1.3·10^7 operations for the maximal input, easily fast.
- Gaussian elimination works on at most N masks, each elimination touches at most the current rank bits → O(N * rank) ≤ O(N^2) in worst case, but with N ≤ 10^4 it is well within limits.
- Memory usage is O(N) for the masks and O(number of distinct primes) for the prime‑to‑bit mapping.

The same idea is implemented in all five languages, using language‑specific fast I/O and appropriate data structures (Python int, C++ bitset, Java BitSet, C array of unsigned long long chunks, JavaScript BigInt).
solution.timeComplexity: O(N * P + N * R) where P ≈ 1229 (number of primes ≤ 10^4) and R is the rank (≤ N). In practice ≤ 2·10^7 operations per test file.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N + D) where D is the number of distinct odd‑exponent primes across the whole test file (≤ N + 1229).
